Five-and-twenty years old, with a patent of nobility that dates from the Crusades, conferring a right to intrude in his sovereign's bedroom and present the shivering King of France with a clean shirt, it is natural that he should be overwhelmed with debt, a bad landlord, an unlucky gamester, a faithless lover, a dishonest politician, and an uncertain friend. 122.metadata_frame: The poem in the following question is drawn from Spicewood, a book of poems published in 1921 by Lizette Woodworth Reese, an American poet and teacher.
122.main_question:
Identify the poetic form of the following work, titled "To Time."
ON some grave afternoon, in years to be,
When I pass down the withering country walks,
Will he be but the scent blown out the stalks
Of the wrecked rose, and nothing more to me?
And I that was?-this wild girl that am I -
But a blurred figure fleeting by the pane?— 
I that do eat my heart out, and am fain
To cast myself down on the clods and die?-
But scent, but mist? I will not have it so.
Set us twain fast in some far weather's round;
Press not for me that wine from vale or hill,
Who drink, forgetting youth and yester go.
You dare not. I would dash the cup to ground.
Hurt me. That I loved once, would I love still.
ground_truths: Petrarchan sonnet.
122.answer: The form of the poem is lyrical.

Lawrence, of virtuous father virtuous son,
Now that the fields are dank, and ways are mire,
Where shall we sometimes meet, and by the fire
Help waste a sullen day, what may be won
From the hard season gaining? Time will run
On smoother, till Favonius reinspire
The frozen earth, and clothe in fresh attire
The lily and rose that neither sowed nor spun.
What neat repast shall feast us, light and choice,
Of Attic taste, with wine, whence we may rise
To hear the lute well touched, or artful voice
Warble immortal notes and Tuscan air?
He who of those delights can judge, and spare
To interpose them oft, is not unwise.

133.metadata_frame: The following question is based on a passage in Beneath Blue Skies and Gray, a book of poems published in 1898 by Ingram Crockett, an American poet and journalist.
133.main_question: Write two rhymed couplets of four-foot iambic verse, using some anapaestic substitution, on the theme of "The Oak Wood.."

When God pronounced his work all good
He must have thought of the old oak wood,
Of the old oak wood on an autumn day
With the sunshine goldening all that way
133.length_spec: lines of verse, as specified in the question
133.answer:
Beneath the oak wood, in summer, I lie,
And watch the white clouds sailing by;
And I think of the years that go fleetingly o'er,
And shall return to me nevermore.
In winter, I stand 'neath the oak wood, and see
The branches all bare, and the leaves dead and dry;
And I think of the years that have passed from me,
And shall never come back, till eternity.
--Ingram Crockett, Beneath Blue Skies and Gray (1898).

I made no friends; as soon as school was done 
I used to trudge off gravely by myself
To lord it in the kingdom of my choice;
A pebbly beach, walled in on every side
139.length_spec: lines of verse, as specified in the question
139.answer:
I loved the sea, and hated men,
And ran away to be alone with it;
And walked upon the shining sands, and watched
The waves come rolling in, and broke, and died;
And saw the white gulls flying in the sky,
And felt myself a part of all I saw.
And so the hours passed by, and I was glad.

The crowds of women in the streets, walking rapidly past, pushing and elbowing everyone who stands in the way, all intent on business or pleasure, are a sight not likely to be soon forgotten. I wonder how people can stand the noise and bustle.
435.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 50–80 words total
435.answer: A traveller's first impression of London is one of vastness. The huge metropolis spreads itself out before him like a sea. He sees a forest of masts and a wilderness of roofs. The noise of its traffic deafens him, and the crowd of its passengers astonishes him. He feels himself to be a mere atom in a mighty whole. But, in course of time, he grows used to the scene, and begins to distinguish a few prominent features in it. He discerns order in the apparent chaos, and symmetry in the apparent irregularity. He perceives that the vast city has a life and a purpose of its own, and that, though it may be long before he can fully comprehend either, he has only to wait, and he will understand both.

514.main_question: What is the origin of the philosophy of animism?
ground_truths: It can hardly be said to be obvious why, in uncultured races or individuals, there should arise that invariable tendency to represent natural forces as conscious and anthropomorphic. Evidently the notion of an animating, separable, and surviving soul commends itself as the ready explanation of many familiar phenomena, and the appropriate instrument of a philosophy which ascribes animation to nature at large.
514.length_spec: two to three sentences, about 40–70 words total
514.answer: Animism is a philosophical system which derives all things from animation, and regards the world as animated. It was first propounded by Thales of Miletus, who flourished about 640 B.C., and was afterwards adopted by many of the Greek philosophers. The animistic school was followed by the Stoics, and passed on to the Fathers of the Christian Church, and to the schoolmen of the Middle Ages. In modern times, it has been maintained by Descartes and other philosophers of France, and by Cudworth, More, and Glanvill, in England.

523.main_question: What can be said about the life and manners of the Afghans?
ground_truths: The Afghans, inured to bloodshed from childhood, are familiar with death, and are audacious in attack, but easily discouraged by failure; excessively turbulent and unsubmissive to law or discipline; apparently frank and affable in manner, especially when they hope to gain some object, but capable of the grossest brutality when that hope ceases.

The first impression made by the Afghans is favourable. The European, especially if he come from India, is charmed by their apparently frank, open-hearted, hospitable, and manly manners; but the charm is not of long duration, and he finds that under this frank demeanour there is craft as inveterate, if not as accomplished, as in any Hindu.
523.length_spec: two to three sentences, about 40–60 words total
523.answer: The Afghans are a fierce and warlike people, inhabiting a country of great natural strength, between Persia and India. They are Sunni Mohammedans, and speak a dialect of Persian. Their life is partly pastoral and partly predatory, and they are estimated to number about five millions.

528.answer: Francis Bacon was born in 1561, and died in 1626. He was educated at Cambridge, and studied law at Gray's Inn. In 1589 he became a member of Parliament, and ten years later was made solicitor-general. He rose to be lord keeper of the great seal in 1613, and was created Baron Verulam in 1618, and Viscount St. Albans in 1620. He was the author of numerous philosophical and scientific works, and of a collection of essays, which have long been popular.

529.answer: Backgammon is an ancient and popular indoor game, played by two persons on a board divided into 64 compartments, with dice and 15 pieces, called men. The game is of great antiquity, and was formerly very fashionable.

Previous to the year 1871 the abuse of the practice of baby-farming in England had grown to an alarming extent, while the trials of Margaret Waters and Mary Hall called attention to the infamous relations between the lying-in houses and the baby-farming houses of London.
530.length_spec: two to three sentences, about 20–80 words total
530.answer: Baby-farming is the practice of taking charge of infants for hire, and bringing them up apart from their parents. The term is popularly applied to any person who receives infants to nurse, but in a legal sense it is restricted to those who receive more than one at a time. The object of the persons so receiving infants is to make a profit by their maintenance, and the practice has been severely condemned, on the ground of the risk to which the health of the children is exposed, and the temptation to which the persons in charge of them are subjected, of substituting dead for living children. The term was first used in 1841, and since then various legislative enactments have been made to check the evil. In 1854 an Act was passed, making it compulsory on all persons receiving infants

532.length_spec: two to three sentences, about 2–70 words total
532.answer: A baboon is a large species of ape, of the genus Cynocephalus, having a short and broad muzzle, a long and powerful tail, and cheek-pouches. The species inhabit the western parts of Africa, from Senegal to the Cape of Good Hope, and are social in their habits, living in troops, which sometimes consist of several hundreds. They feed on fruits and insects, and are very destructive to young plants. The females have two, and the males generally four, young ones at a birth. The name 'baboon' is of uncertain etymology, but is probably of imitative origin.

561.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a short passage from Round the World in Silence, a travelogue published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American writer. Try to approximate the likely perspective and style of that source.
561.main_question: How might a traveller describe Cairo?
ground_truths: Cairo is a fascinating place. It would be long before I should tire of watching the life on the streets. Arabs, in long flowing robes, Jews, Mohammedans, tall black men from the Soudan, English soldiers, officers, English ladies, donkeys, tram cars, and handsome carriages all mingle in confusion.
561.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 50–80 words total
561.answer: Cairo is a city of white buildings, green fields, and blue skies. It stands in the midst of a great plain, and the Nile flows past it to the sea. In the season of inundation the plain is converted into a vast lake, and the city becomes an island. All the year round it is a place of life and bustle. The busiest time is in the spring, when pilgrims from all parts of the Mohammedan world congregate to the great annual fair. At other times it is a tranquil haunt of learning, and a centre of art. The citadel on the heights is a strong fortress, and below it lies the native town, with its bazaars, mosques, and minarets. In the winter the European quarter spreads along the banks of the river, and

562.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a short passage from Round the World in Silence, a travelogue published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American writer. Try to approximate the likely perspective and style of that source.
562.main_question: Write a first-person account of the difficulties a traveller might encounter managing their money in India.
ground_truths: I am getting to understand the money at last. The banks cash our American Express Company's cheques with English gold in sovereigns. You can imagine how heavy twenty of these are. In Italy we could get paper money, and go to the bank often; but in India I have twice already had my funds run almost to zero before I could get a chance to replenish, so I find it necessary to carry large sums.
562.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 40–90 words total
562.answer: I found the currency perplexing, for Indian silver and copper coins are of many denominations, and I had to be continually reducing my rupees and annas to pence. The task was further complicated by the fact that the value of the rupee fluctuates, and I had to learn not only how many pence it was worth, but also to what extent it had changed in value since the previous day.

563.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a short passage from Round the World in Silence, a travelogue published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American writer. Try to approximate the likely perspective and style of that source.
563.main_question: Describe the experience of being a foreigner in Japan.
ground_truths: Tourists are very rare in these parts, and all the little brown people along the way showed great interest in us. Tiny boys on the road took off their caps, mothers turned their babies around to see the strange sight, old people, going home hand in hand from their day's work in the fields, called out a cheerful greeting.
563.length_spec: two to three sentences, about 50–80 words total
563.answer: I was conscious of being a foreigner in Japan, first and foremost, because the language was strange to me, and because the ways of the people were not my ways. I found myself living in a world which was foreign and yet familiar. The strangeness was in small things, but the familiarity in great. The strangeness was in the paper walls of the houses, in the paper soles of the shoes, in the paper umbrellas, in the paper lanterns, in the paper windows; the familiarity was in the broad green fields, in the great trees, in the mountain shapes, in the blue skies, and in the quiet sea.

564.metadata_frame: The following question is drawn from Round the World in Silence, a travelogue published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American writer. The best answer will approximate the perspective and style of that source.
564.main_question: How might a traveller describe a journey from Cairo to see the Pyramids?
ground_truths: All the while the distant Pyramids lured us on. These are known as the Pyramids of Ghizeh. At the end of the car line, a crowd of Egyptians with camels and donkeys were in waiting, and our guide soon had us all mounted on camels with a boy to lead each beast. I had a nice, clean, intelligent-looking boy, but I approached the camel allotted me with some trepidation.
564.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 60–90 words total
564.answer: A traveller might describe a journey from Cairo to see the Pyramids as a ride across the desert in an easy-going carriage, broken only by an hour’s halt at the base of Cheops, to contemplate that mighty monument, and then on again to Gizeh, and so to the capital.

565.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a short passage from Round the World in Silence, a travelogue published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American writer. Try to approximate the likely perspective and style of that source.
565.main_question: Describe street life in the city of Colombo.
ground_truths: There is a "pull" about the place that is irresistible. It is embowered in green trees and shrubs, with fragrant flowers everywhere; the roads are fine and hard, the streets are lighted with gas, but most bewitching of all are the people. They are so quaint as to clothes, or lack of them, so bright-eyed and smiling.
565.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 50–80 words total
565.answer: Colombo is a city of bustle and animation. Its streets are thronged with people of many races and colours, all busy and intent upon their own affairs. Cingalese, Tamils, and Moors move to and fro, chattering in their various tongues; white men walk quickly, or drive in carriages or motors; and here and there a solitary Buddhist priest goes by in yellow robes, meditating upon the vanity of human wishes. The shops are full of curiosities; and the bazaars are crowded with Eastern merchandise. The air is lively with the clang of tram-bells, and the clatter of hoofs. The scene is animated, vivid, and interesting.

662.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question is drawn from Centennial Gleanings, a book of poems published in 1876 by Kate McCarthy, an American writer.

ground_truths:
'Tis at your bidding, Fate, I come
My hist'ry to recount;
And I, though unrenowned by age,
Am Liberty's sweet fount.
Before a white man's foot had trod
Upon my long-sought shore,
E'en though the savage only knew,
'Twas rich in golden ore.
662.length_spec: lines of verse, as specified in the question
662.answer:
In the red man’s hunting grounds I stood,
Ere white men o’er the waters came;
And, free and peaceful, then the wood
Was vocal with my name.
I heard the forest children sing,
And thought not of a tyrant king.
II. ⁎I saw the mighty forest fall,
And fields smile in the sun;
And, where the wigwam stood, was built
A hamlet fair to see;
And spires rose, and a voice was heard,
And I was christen’d Liberty.
--KATE MCCARTHY.

Then more in detail he describes the various psychoses classified mainly according to their general immediate exciting causes. Each one is discussed as to its general characteristics and symptoms with some reference to its etiology, in short with a brief summary of the appearance and manifestation of each, with its prognosis and suggestions for treatment.

707.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a passage from A Day in the Moon. This is a book of astronomical speculation by the French astronomer and meteorologist Abbe Moreux, first published in 1912.
707.main_question: Will it ever be possible to travel to the Moon? Answer in a short paragraph.
ground_truths: At present, travel to the moon is impossible. But perhaps some centuries to come the problem will be solved. Before the invention of balloons and aeroplanes, after all, no one thought seriously of being able to rise in the air. Whoever finds out a means of suppressing or even diminishing the force of gravitation, that mysterious power which holds the heavenly bodies together and keeps us fixed to the earth, will thus enable men to leave their planet and launch out upon the conquest of neighbouring worlds. And the Moon may be the first stage of these inter-planetary voyages.
707.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 40–100 words total
707.answer: It will never be possible to travel to the Moon, because the Moon has no atmosphere, and consequently no life. Moreover, the force of gravity on its surface is only one-sixth of what it is on the earth, so that a man weighing 150 pounds here would weigh only 30 pounds there, and could not stand upright, but would be obliged to spend his whole time in crawling about like a fly. Lastly, the temperature must be perpetually below freezing-point, and all waters would be solidified into ice.

708.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a passage from A Day in the Moon. This is a book of astronomical speculation by the French astronomer and meteorologist Abbe Moreux, first published in 1912.
708.main_question: In a short paragraph, vividly describe the passage from night to day on the Moon.
ground_truths: It is still night in the region in which we have landed on the surface of the Moon. The sky sparkles with incomparable splendour. The eye can distinguish in it thousands and thousands of stars. Not even the glories of a tropical night can give any idea of what night on the Moon must be. But soon the Sun will appear. There is no dawn to herald it, but already there is rising in the east a long, spindle-shaped trail of whiteness; it is the zodiacal light, ten times more brilliant than with us. For hours this false dawn rises higher in the sky; its tints of phosphorescent colours are brighter and brighter from the summit to the base; in the midst of the enormous cone, all aglow with green and opal, there gleams a resplendent star: it is the beautiful Venus, hardly recognisable amid its mantle of light. But already the fine rays of the Sun's corona are heralding the day-star.
708.length_spec: a short paragraph, about 100–150 words total
708.answer: At midnight the moon is dark and featureless; but, as the earth rolls eastward, and the lunar day begins, light falls upon it from the east. Craters and peaks grow gradually visible, and the outlines of plains become distinct. By and by the shadows vanish, and the whole surface is illuminated. At sunrise the illumination is complete, and the aspect of the lunar day is fully revealed. The sunshine kindles a red glow over the desolate scene, which deepens into orange, and then fades away as the earth-borrowed rays pass westward. At sunset the colours disappear, and the moon becomes pale and ghost-like, as at first. Night falls upon it, and it is hidden from view.

709.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a passage from A Day in the Moon. This is a book of astronomical speculation by the French astronomer and meteorologist Abbé Moreux, first published in 1912.
709.main_question: Are the lunar craters formed by volcanoes? In a short paragraph, explain what we know about the truth of this theory.
ground_truths: It is likely that there are in the Moon some small craters formed in this way. But the volcanic theory of the lunar circles does not explain everything. One might, first of all, object that it is very strange that we generally do not find in the interior of these great circles any serious inequalities of the ground, any mountain resembling those of the outer rampart. One would expect to find regular concentric rings at various distances from the centre, marking the progressive decrease of the eruptive phenomena in the course of geological time. But there is nothing of the kind!
709.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 60–100 words total
709.answer: Volcanic craters are probably to be found on the moon. The telescope has revealed a number of them, and the spectroscope shows that they eject lava. But the craters are not numerous, and the lunar volcanoes must be feeble. Moreover, the lunar volcanoes seem to have long been extinct.

710.metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Ethics for Children: a Guide for Teachers and Parents, a conduct book written by Ella Lyman Cabot, an American philosopher and writer. It was published in 1910.
710.main_question: Why are children troublesome, or, as we crudely and falsely term it, "bad"? Answer in a short paragraph.
ground_truths: Usually, from one of two reasons. They lack vitality, or they overflow with uncontrolled vitality. Fretfulness, laziness, cowardice, lying, and even aggressive faults like perversity and obstinacy, are often due to lack of vitality—a pathetic, misjudged protest against being forced into the wrong work. Bad temper, cruelty, roughness, stealing, and all the myriad acts we classify as "mischievous," may result from the great gift of superabundant vitality—an energy which, like electricity, is capable of service, but disastrous when uncontrolled.
710.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 70–90 words total
710.answer: Children are troublesome because they are learning to be men and women, and all learning is troublesome. The period of childhood is the period of preparation for life, and life is never easy. The child must walk before he can run, and he must speak before he can talk plainly. So, in learning to be a human being, he must first acquire the power of behaving, and then practise it, before he can behave well. Behaving well is not easy, but behaving ill is difficult. Behaving ill is troublesome to the child himself, and annoying to those about him.
